description: Architectural patterns available for constraint-driven decision making

patterns.name: Bijection / Invertible Mapping
patterns.description: One-to-one mapping where every input maps to a unique output AND every output maps back to a unique input. No information is lost.
patterns.when_to_use: When you need exact reversibility — encode then decode, encrypt then decrypt, serialize then deserialize with zero loss.
weaknesses: domain and codomain must have same cardinality
patterns.exemplar: Feistel cipher in Library of Babel — chose bijection because every page index must map to exactly one page and back

patterns.name: Idempotent Operation
patterns.description: Operation that produces the same result regardless of how many times it is applied. f(f(x)) = f(x).
patterns.when_to_use: Retry logic, message processing, state synchronization, API endpoints that may be called multiple times.
weaknesses: harder to implement for complex mutations
forbidden_when: each invocation must produce different results
patterns.exemplar: HTTP PUT is idempotent — uploading the same file twice yields the same result

patterns.name: Monotonic Accumulation
patterns.description: Values only grow (or only shrink), never reverse direction. Append-only logs, version counters, intelligence levels.
patterns.when_to_use: When history must be preserved, when rollback is dangerous, when you need to prove 'this only gets better'.
patterns.exemplar: MetaBlooms intelligence_level — only increases as mastery definitions, decisions, and lessons accumulate

patterns.name: Fail-Closed Gate
patterns.description: On any unexpected condition, deny/halt rather than permit/continue. Default to 'no' rather than 'yes'.
patterns.when_to_use: Security gates, quality gates, admission decisions, anything where false-positive (allowing bad input) is worse than false-negative (rejecting good input).
forbidden_when: availability is more important than correctness
patterns.exemplar: MPP phases — any CRITICAL finding triggers FAIL CLOSED, halting the pipeline

patterns.name: Constraint-Driven Elimination
patterns.description: Instead of picking the 'best' option, define constraints and eliminate everything that violates them. Selection emerges from what survives.
patterns.when_to_use: Architectural decisions, tool selection, any choice where you need justified, traceable reasoning rather than gut feel.
weaknesses: can eliminate all candidates if constraints are too tight
patterns.exemplar: Feistel cipher selection — hash eliminated (no reverse), LCG eliminated (fragile), lookup eliminated (impossible), Feistel survived

patterns.name: Recursive Refinement with Convergence Bounds
patterns.description: Iteratively improve output by detecting defects and fixing them, with hard limits on iterations and convergence testing to prevent infinite loops.
patterns.when_to_use: Quality improvement, document editing, code review cycles — any iterative process that could loop forever without bounds.
patterns.exemplar: RRP: BUILD -> EVALUATE -> REWRITE, max 2 iterations, convergence test: defects_after >= defects_before -> STOP

patterns.name: Evidence-Before-Execution Gate
patterns.description: Refuse to execute until sufficient evidence has been gathered for all claims. Prevents action based on assumptions.
patterns.when_to_use: When the cost of acting on false premises is high — any decision with significant consequences.
weaknesses: can block action when evidence is genuinely unavailable
forbidden_when: speed is critical and stakes are low
patterns.exemplar: MPP Phase 2.75 PREPARATION_GATE — blocks BUILD if mastery definition has OPEN knowledge gaps

patterns.name: Lesson Promotion Lifecycle
patterns.description: Observations get promoted through levels (OBSERVATION -> HYPOTHESIS -> CONSTRAINT -> INVARIANT) only with evidence at each step. Prevents premature generalization.
patterns.when_to_use: Learning systems, knowledge management, any system that needs to distinguish between 'I noticed this once' and 'this is a law'.
patterns.exemplar: MetaBlooms assimilation: MMD findings become observations, recurring observations become hypotheses, confirmed hypotheses become constraints, mechanically-checked constraints become invariants
